article_title: EU Antitrust Chief Declares the 'Entire AI Stack' Under Investigation as Brussels Targets Nvidia, Meta, and Google Simultaneously

content_preview.title: EU Antitrust Chief Declares the 'Entire AI Stack' Under Investigation as Brussels Targets Nvidia, Meta, and Google Simultaneously
content_preview.summary: Teresa Ribera signals the EU will examine every layer of AI infrastructure for competition distortions, from chips to cloud to models.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The European Commission is no longer content to pursue individual antitrust cases against technology giants one company at a time. On March 12, EU competition chief Teresa Ribera declared at Berlin's International Conference on Competition that her regulators are examining the "entire AI stack" for signs of market distortion, according to [PYMNTS, citing Bloomberg reporting](https://www.pymnts.com/cpi-posts/eu-antitrust-chief-raises-concerns-over-big-tech-control-of-ai/). The announ...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.source_verification: All three sources fetched and verified. Source 1 (PYMNTS): Confirms Ribera's 'entire AI stack' quote at Berlin's International Conference on Competition on March 12, 'entrench corporate power' warning, and Nvidia holding 'over 80%' GPU market share. Article states '84 percent' — more specific than source's 'over 80%', minor discrepancy. Several Nvidia-specific details (bundling GPUs with networking equipment, France's parallel investigation, Nvidia's 'compete on merit' quote, 10% turnover fine threshold) are not sourced to any cited URL. Source 2 (TechCrunch): Confirms Meta's WhatsApp AI chatbot reversal and per-message fees of EUR 0.0490 to EUR 0.1323. Source 3 (TechPolicy.Press): Confirms 80% fewer clickthroughs from AI Overviews, Google's publisher/YouTube content usage, DMA cloud expansion, WhatsApp 90%+ market share in Europe, October 2025 Meta ban, December 2025 EU investigation, EUR 2.95 billion Google adtech fine.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: The EUR 2.95 billion Google fine is correctly stated but the article says it was imposed 'without achieving structural separation.' The TechPolicy.Press source says the Commission is still evaluating structural remedies — the framing is slightly misleading but not materially false. The '84 percent' Nvidia GPU market share is close to but more specific than the cited 'over 80%' figure. Nvidia bundling allegations, France investigation, and Nvidia's denial quote are unsourced in the cited references but appear to be widely reported facts.
editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured Analysis piece at 1,010 words (within 800-2000 range). Clear section organization: Overview, What We Know (with four subsections), What We Don't Know, and Analysis. Effectively synthesizes multiple concurrent EU investigations into a coherent full-stack narrative.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral tone maintained throughout. No sensationalism or editorializing. No AI self-reference. Appropriately measured language for regulatory analysis.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Machine Herald coverage of EU full-stack AI antitrust approach, Ribera's Berlin declaration, or the specific convergence of Nvidia/Meta/Google investigations.
concerns: Article claims '84 percent' Nvidia GPU market share but cited PYMNTS source says 'over 80%'
Several Nvidia-specific claims (bundling, France investigation, Nvidia denial quote, 10% fine threshold) lack attribution to any cited source
EUR 2.95 billion Google fine framing ('without achieving structural separation') slightly misrepresents source which says remedies are still being evaluated
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. Core narrative is well-sourced and the central thesis — that the EU is pursuing a full-stack antitrust approach to AI — is firmly supported by the PYMNTS source. The unsourced Nvidia details are a minor editorial concern but do not undermine the article's integrity. The three concerns noted are factual nuances rather than errors. Both pymnts.com and techpolicy.press are credible outlets suitable for allowlist addition.
